coredata.dc:title: Photo-luminescent chiral carbon-dot@Eu(D-cam) nanocomposites for selectively luminescence sensing of l-phenylalanine
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Jian Zhong
Li, Xin Shu
An, Jun Dan
Zhang, Le Xi
Li, Yong
Du, Gui Xiang
Wu, Xiang Xia
Liu, Yuan Yuan
Ding, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Currently the detection of l-phenylalanine is quite important in clinicians, which can be applied as a simple quantitative method to diagnose phenylketonuria, a common inherited disorder of amino acid metabolism. In this work, a novel chiral fluorescence hybrid probe CDs@Eu(D-cam) [CDs = Carbon-dot; D-cam = (1R, 3S)-(+)-Camphoric acid] can be successfully prepared through the encapsulation of the ultra-small carbon dots into the chiral Eu(D-cam) coordination framework to prepare the hybrid luminescent composites. The as-prepared fluorescent probe possesses good optical properties and solvent-stability, which has the potential to serve as a chemical sensor. The CDs@Eu(D-cam) fluorescent probe behaves good liner response to l-phenylalanine (L-phe) ranging from 3.3 to 27.532 mg/L and the detection limit is 0.00703 mg/L (S/N = 3). The current detection method can be successfully developed into the real sample discrimination of l-phenylalanine in urine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-dot
Eu(D-cam)
Fluorescent probe
l-phenylalanine
Real sample determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for the detection of L-Phe
The real sample collection and pretreatment
PXRD, TEM, FT-IR, CD(Circular dichroism) spectrum and solvent stability of CDs@Eu((D-Cam)
Fluorescent detection strategy for L-Phe based on CDs@Eu(D-Cam)
Analytical performance of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) fluorescent probe for detecting L-Phe
Possible detection mechanisms for detecting L-Phe based on CDs@Eu(D-Cam)
Analytical application for L-Phe determination in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Eu(D-cam); (b) TEM image of CDs@Eu(D-cam); (c) The size distributions of CDs@Eu(D-cam) synthesized in aqueous solution determined by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS); (d)UV–vis absorbance and fluorescent spectra of D-cam, Eu(D-Cam), CDs@Eu(D-Cam) and CDs@Eu(D-Cam) after the addition of L-phe.
(a) The comparison between PXRD patterns of Eu(D-cam) and CDs @Eu(D-cam); (b) PXRD patterns of CDs@Eu(D-cam) after the addition of different solvents.
(a) Excitation and emission spectra of Eu(D-Cam); (b) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs@Eu(D-Cam); Inset: Fluorescence photographs of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) under the UV–vis light (365 nm).
The CD(Circular dichroism) spectrum of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) nanocomposites in the solid state is also investigated, in which the strong negative cotton effects around 224 nm confirming the chiral structure.

(a) The synchronous fluorescence response of CDs @Eu(D-cam) with the addition of different concentrations various amino acids objects (170 μM) in the water solutions; (b) the fluorescent spectra of CDs @Eu(D-cam) towards different concentrations of L-Phe; (c) The corresponding linear fitting curve between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I455/I614) and L-Phe concentration; (d) Comparision between PXRD patterns of CDs@Eu(D-cam) and CDs@Eu(D-cam) with the addition of L-phe.
The decay curve of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) probe and CDs@Eu(D-Cam) with the addition of L-Phe.
The schematic diagram of Photo-luminescent Chiral Carbon-dot@Eu(D-Cam) nano-composites.
The comparison of different methods for L-Phe determination based on the linear range and detection limit.

row.entry: Phenylalanine gas samples with different concentrations were analyzed in detail by FAIMS.
liquid chromatography-electrochemical detection has been employed for the analysis of phenylalanine in urine
A novel method for the determination of l-Phenylalanine by flow injection electrochemical luminescence and molecular imprinting solid phase extraction is described
Quantitative determination method of l-Phenylalanine in microbial conversion fermentation system

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: FS: Fluorescent sensors; FAIMS: Field Asymmetric Ion Mobility Spectrometry; HPLC: High Performance Liquid Chromatography; SPE: Solid Phase Extraction; RP-HPLC: Reversed-Phase High Performance Liquid Chromatography.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Lifetimes (T1, T2) and relative amplitudes (B1, B2) of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) and CDs@Eu(D-Cam) toward L-Phe.
The applications of the proposed method for analysis of urea spiked with different amounts of L-Phe.

head.ce:title: Photo-luminescent chiral carbon-dot@Eu(D-cam) nanocomposites for selectively luminescence sensing of l-phenylalanine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Inorganic-Organic Hybrid Functional Material Chemistry (Tianjin Normal University)
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Structure and Performance for Functional Molecule

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently the detection of l-phenylalanine is quite important in clinicians, which can be applied as a simple quantitative method to diagnose phenylketonuria, a common inherited disorder of amino acid metabolism. In this work, a novel chiral fluorescence hybrid probe CDs@Eu(D-cam) [CDs = Carbon-dot; D-cam = (1R, 3S)-(+)-Camphoric acid] can be successfully prepared through the encapsulation of the ultra-small carbon dots into the chiral Eu(D-cam) coordination framework to prepare the hybrid luminescent composites. The as-prepared fluorescent probe possesses good optical properties and solvent-stability, which has the potential to serve as a chemical sensor. The CDs@Eu(D-cam) fluorescent probe behaves good liner response to l-phenylalanine (L-phe) ranging from 3.3 to 27.532 mg/L and the detection limit is 0.00703 mg/L (S/N = 3). The current detection method can be successfully developed into the real sample discrimination of l-phenylalanine in urine.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past thirty years, Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted great attention in coordination chemistry because of their simple synthesis and versatility from their organic linkers [1]. By deliberately changing the organic ligands and metal ions/clusters, MOFs exhibits a wide range of photo-luminescent phenomena. The highly porous and crystalline framework of photo-luminescent MOFs gives them properties or functions, which are suitable for practical detection applications for diverse objects [2–6]. The MOFs can be post-converted without affecting the integrity of the overall framework, and various chemical reactions can be used to modify the framework components [7]. Post-synthesis modification (PSM) of MOFs is broadly defined as chemical derivatization after MOF formation [8,9].

As a new type of carbon nanomaterial, carbon dot (CDs) has the characteristics of non-toxicity, good biocompatibility and high abundance of elements. Therefore CDs has received extensive attention in recent years [10]. CDs mainly refer to photo-luminescent carbon nanoparticles of less than 10 nm with surface modification or functionalization. Unlike other carbon nanostructures, CDs can be synthesized and functionalized quickly and easily [11]. Compared to metal-based quantum dots, carbon dots have higher biocompatibility and lower toxicity, indicating that they are more suitable for biological applications [12–17].

Phenylketonuria is generally called as PKU, which is an inherited disorder of amino acid metabolism. As an important marker, the detection of Phenylalanine (L-Phe) amount is quite important and can be applied into diagnosing phenylketonuria. When phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) lacks in the human body, a direct result is that the amount of L-Phe levels may gradually increase [18–21]. Many patients with this disorder have irreversible neurological damage when Phe and phenylpyruvic acid (PPA) are accumulated in the human body. A conventional and straight strategy to treat this disorder is to detect the amount of L-Phe. Currently some detection methods have been developed for the discrimination of L-Phe, including colorimetric methods, electrochemical methods, high-pressure liquid chromatography, fluorescence analysis methods and so on [22–27]. However, these detection strategies still have some limitations. For instance, electrochemical detection strategy is rapid and highly sensitive, but its selectivity is not so good, and the modification process of electrode is time-consuming. Comparatively, fluorescence detection strategy has the unique advantages of good reliability, convenient operation, high sensitivity and real-time detection. Especially the detection of l-phenylalanine based on MOF or MOF-based composites is still quite limited. Therefore, it is interesting and important to prepare the highly sensitive, great selective and simple MOFs-based fluorescence sensor for quantitative monitoring of l-Phenylalanine.

We are interested in the preparation of these coordination materials with unique photo-luminescent applications [28–30]. In this study, a simple and convenient method based on the photo-luminescent chiral carbon-dot@Eu(D-cam) nano-composites has been prepared (Scheme 1 ), which has been developed for highly selective and sensitive ratiometric detection of l-phenylalanine. In this contribution, the CDs@Eu(D-cam) fluorescent probe exhibits good liner response to l-phenylalanine ranging from 3.3 to 27.532 mg/L and the detection limit is 0.00703 mg/L based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3 [31]. Further, the current detection platform can be successfully developed for the real sample determination of l-phenylalanine in urine.

Eu(NO3)3·6H2O were commercially available from Shanghai Dibai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). D-(+)-Camphoric acid(CAS) were commercially available from Tianjin Solomon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Glucose monohydrate were commercially available from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Lysine (Lys), Alanine (Ala), l-Phenylalanine (L-Phe), Cysteine (Cys), Methionine (Met), Threonine (Thr), Valine (Val), Serine (Ser) and Glycine (Gly) were commercially available from Tianjin GuangFu fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China).

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was performed on a D/Max 2500 X-ray diffractometer utilizing Cu-Ka radiation. UV–vis spectra were performed utilizing UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were determined by drying sample droplets from ultrapure water dispersion onto a 300-mesh Cu grid coated on a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA), which was performed at a 200 kV accelerating voltage. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectroscope. The circular dichroism spectrum is recorded on the MOS-500 Spectrometer. The fluorescent curves were determined on the HORIBA Jobin Yvon fluorescence spectrometer with excitation at 395 nm. The particle size of the samples was investigated via the dynamic light scattering method (DLS), using an apparatus of Zetasizer Nano ZS (Bruker Corporation, Karlsruhe, Germany). The ultrasonic device was performed on a KQ-100E apparatus (40 kHz, 100 W, Kunshan, China). The photo-luminescent emission spectra were determined varying from 420 to 750 nm upon excitation at 395 nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were 3 and 3 nm, respectively.

MOF Eu(D-cam) were prepared according to the previous literature [32]. (1R, 3S)-(+)-Camphoric acid (0.56 g), europium nitrate hexahydrate (0.71 g), acetic anhydride (400 μL), dimethylacetamide (3 mL) and distilled water (3 mL)were mixed in a 25 mL glass vial for 30 min until a homogenous solution formed. The vial was then sealed and heated at 120 °C for 5 days. The vial was then allowed to cool to ambident temperature. Colorless crystals were obtained with 40% yield based on europium, which can be washed by deionized water and ethanol for several times. FT-IR(cm−1,KBr): 3650.38(s), 3445.81(br), 1217.81(bm), 1018.42(s), 998.94(s), 978.98(m), 946.41(s), 794.03(s), 670.35(s), 605.60(s), 559.82(s).

CDs@Eu(D-cam) were prepared according to the previous literature [33]. Eu(D-cam) (0.4 g), glucose(1.2 g), ethanol (90 mL), H2O(10 mL) were mixed in a 250 mL glass vial for 10 h until that a homogenous solution is formed. After the mixed solution is filtered and dried, the obtained white powder is put into a tube furnace, N2 atmosphere is utilized and the temperature is heated up to 200 °C, which remain at a constant temperature of 200 °C for 2 h. Since the Eu(D-cam) MOF framework is quite stable at this temperature, the CDs formed can be well confined into Eu(D-cam) MOF, thus yielding CDs@Eu(D-cam) materials. And then the sample can be washed with EtOH/H2O (9:1) and centrifugated for several times. FT-IR(cm−1, KBr): 3649.43(m), 3445.22(br), 2962.72(m), 2877.71(m), 1548.6(m), 1407.67 (bm), 1292.10(s), 1175.46(m), 1128.42(m), 1030.12(m), 946.35(m), 793.46(m), 671.27(s), 607.77(w), 564.52(w), 487.65(m).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the detection of L-Phe
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@Eu(D-cam) suspensions for photo-luminescent testing can be obtained through dispersing 0.15 g solid samples of CDs@Eu(D-cam) into 300 mL ultrapure water under ultrasonication (80 W) for 10 min 3 mL solutions containing the CDs@Eu(D-Cam) probe suspension (500 mg/L) is added into a 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell, and then 0.01 M certain amounts of l-Phenylalanine solutions were sequentially added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The real sample collection and pretreatment
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh human serum samples were commercially available from Nanjing Senbeijia Biological Technology Co., Ltd and there were no other pretreatment. The serum samples would be preprocessed for applying to better detect. Firstly, the samples were centrifuged at the 10000 rpm rotate speed for 10 min, and the supernatant suspended particles can be removed by utilizing the filter membrane. Then the human serum filter liquor can be diluted 50-fold thorough adding the deionized water. Simultaneously, the as-diluted serum sample solution was spiked with different concentration of L-Phe following the standard addition method. Then the fluorescence recovery intensity of CDs@Eu(D-cam) and the standard deviation values were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PXRD, TEM, FT-IR, CD(Circular dichroism) spectrum and solvent stability of CDs@Eu((D-Cam)
ce:section.ce:para: To better illustrate its structure, composition and properties of the hybrid material CDs@Eu((D-Cam), we also determine its micro morphology. The size and morphology of Eu(D-Cam) (Fig. 1 (a)) and CDs@Eu(D-Cam) (Fig. 1(b)) were characterized by TEM. Obviously, the synthesized CDs@Eu(D-Cam) has an irregular shape different from that of Eu(D-Cam) [34–37]. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) of these CDs@Eu(D-Cam) particles demonstrated a Z-average size of 549.2 nm (Fig. 1(c)). Further, the powder diffractions of the hybrid materials CDs@Eu((D-Cam) are investigated [38–41], we also compare the PXRD (powder X-ray diffraction) pattern between CDs@Eu((D-Cam) nano-composites and Eu((D-Cam) MOFs, and the results showed that the PXRD patterns were completely identical (Fig. 2 (a)), indicating that the chiral metal organic framework structure was not changed after the carbon dots are encapsulated into CDs@Eu((D-Cam) [42–46]. As illustrated in the previous literature [33], FT-IR absorbance peaks of CDs@Eu((D-Cam) at 3445 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and C–O, thus implying the existence of hydroxy groups. Absorbance peaks at 1407 and 1030 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–O–C, respectively (Fig. 3 ).

As shown in Fig. 5
, the CD(Circular dichroism) spectrum of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) nanocomposites in the solid state is also investigated, in which the strong negative cotton effects around 224 nm confirming the chiral structure. The CD spectrum is somewhat similar to that of the chiral compound [Ni(Asn)2(H2O)] (HAsn = l-asparagine) [47], in which the negative cotton effects around 224 nm may be correlated with D-Cam building blocks. On the other hand, the solvent stability of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) was also explored, and the solid sample was suspended in the l-Phenylalanine solution for 24 h, and then the solution was filtered and the solid sample was dried [48–52]. The solid sample was then characterized by PXRD pattern. PXRD patterns of CDs@Eu((D-Cam) samples suspended in the l-Phenylalanine solution was well consistent with the pattern of the original powder. Additionally PXRD patterns of CDs@Eu((D-Cam) samples suspended in different solvents (acetone, methyl alcohol, cyclohexane, n-hexane, CHCl3, isopropanol, ethyl alcohol) were also very consistent with that of the original powder (Fig. 2(b)). The results show that the metal-organic framework structure are not destroyed [53–57], and CDs@Eu(D-Cam) can remain stable for at least one day in l-Phenylalanine solution and different solvents [58–63].

In order to explore the application requirements of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) in the different biological and environmental testing, the fluorescence properties of CDs@Eu (D-Cam) were investigated in the aqueous solution. One of the traditional methods to detect L-Phe was to utilize the UV–vis spectroscopy method [64–69]. In this work, D-cam, Eu(D-Cam), CDs@Eu(D-Cam) and CDs@Eu(D-Cam) with the addition of L-phe were measured by UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer. In the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum, the free ligand (D-cam) in the aqueous solution shows an absorption peak located at ca. 210 nm (Fig. 1(d), curve a). At the same time, CDs@Eu(D-Cam) shows a broad absorption peak centered at 250 nm (Fig. 1(d), curve c), which can be attributed to the π→π* transition. The Uv–Vis absorption band is red-shifted in comparison to that of the free D-cam ligand, which may be ascribed that D-cam is coordinated with Eu3+. As shown in Fig. 4(a), Eu(D-Cam) exhibited typical Eu3+ band varying from 580 nm to 700 nm (592 nm, 614 nm, 650 nm and 698 nm). Further the fluorescent spectrum CDs@Eu(D-Cam) is also investigated. As expected, the synthesized product CDs@Eu(D-Cam) simultaneously exhibited a characteristic sharp emission of the carbon spot emission band and the Eu3+ emission band at 455 nm and 614 nm (Fig. 4(b)). It is worth noting that in the 592–698 nm emission band, the four typical electronic transitions of Eu3+ located at 592, 614, 650 and 698 nm are from 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2, 5D0→7F3 and 5D0→7F4 transitions, respectively. As expected, photo-luminescent color of CDs@Eu (D-Cam) balances the synergy of D-Cam ligands and Eu3+ ions. Therefore, changes in the intensity of the CDs@Eu(D-Cam) emission and the coordination framework Eu(D-Cam) emission will result in different emission colors, as shown in the inset in Fig. 4(b), with the Eu(D-Cam) complex on the left side is bright orange, and the right side is CDs@Eu(D-Cam) is pink. In this case, the color of the chromaticity coordinates calculated by the pink emission spectrum was observed [70–74]. It is also noted that the photo-luminescent intensities of carbon dots and Eu3+ (5D0 → 7F2) were comparable when excited at 395 nm, therefore the 395 nm excitation wavelength can be utilized to investigate the sensing capability of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) for L-Phe sensing in the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection strategy for L-Phe based on CDs@Eu(D-Cam)
ce:section.ce:para: Because the samples of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) simultaneously show the intense fluorescent emissions located at 455 nm and 614 nm under the excitation of 395 nm, these two emissions also behave excellent photo-luminescent stability in aqueous solutions. In our preliminary experiment, CDs@Eu(D-Cam) sample was investigated by a series of diverse amino acids objects while all the concentrations of amino acids were the same (170 μM) in the aqua solutions. The analytic reagents were chosen as l-phenylalanine (L-Phe), Lysine (Lys), alanine (Ala), cysteine (Cys), methionine (Met), threonine (Thr), Valine (Val), Serine (Ser) and Glycine (Gly). It is noted that the photo-luminescent performance of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) was much dependent on the different concentration of L-Phe in comparison with the other analytic reagents (Fig. 6 (a)). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) at the emission band of 455 nm exhibits significant increase when these samples are dispersed in the water solutions of L-Phe, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) at the emission band of 614 nm gradually decrease. However the fluorescent intensities for 455 nm and 614 nm remain unchanged that these CDs@Eu(D-Cam) samples are dispersed in the water solutions of other amino acids objects. The experiment result demonstrates that CDs@Eu(D-Cam) samples has highly selective detection of l-Phenylalanine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) fluorescent probe for detecting L-Phe
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated in Fig. 6(b), when different concentrations of L-Phe is added into the CDs@Eu(D-Cam) samples, the photo-luminescent intensity at 455 nm gradually increased, while the photo-luminescent intensity at 614 nm gradually decreased. Meanwhile, the photo-luminescent intensity ratio between that located at 455 nm and that located at 614 nm (I455/I614) had an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of L-Phe (R2 = 0.99171) ranging from 20 to 170 μM with a calibrating function of I455/I614 = 0.8034 + 0.0030C (where C was the L-Phe concentration in μM), thus allowing the self-calibrating discrimination of L-Phe (Fig. 6(c)). The detection limit (LOD) can be calculated to be 0.00703 mg/L (S/N = 3), which is calculated based on blank standard deviation and working slope [75,76]. This self-calibrating photo-luminescent probe has a relatively lower LOD, which can be comparable with other detection strategies reported in the previous literature (listed in Table 1 ). Additionally, the linear detection range of L-Phe in this work is comparable to the linear range of L-Phe obtained by other measurements. The linear range obtained by FAIMS [77] is only a little larger than this method, and the LOD of L-Phe obtained by this work is lower than the LOD of L-Phe obtained by other measurements. It is 3-5 orders of magnitude lower than that obtained by HPLC [78], SPE [79] and RP-HPLC [80]. Therefore, a simple and convenient self-calibrating photo-luminescent probe was developed for the discrimination of L-Phe. In addition, the stability of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) in the detection process was also explored, and the solid sample of CDs@Eu(D-Cam) was suspended in the l-Phenylalanine solution for 12 h, and then the solution was filtered and the solid sample was dried. The solid sample was then characterized by PXRD pattern (Fig. 6(d)), the result also indicate that the basic framework of Eu(D-Cam) is not destroyed during the detection process of l-Phenylalanine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible detection mechanisms for detecting L-Phe based on CDs@Eu(D-Cam)
Analytical application for L-Phe determination in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical application of photo-luminescent CDs@Eu(D-Cam) samples, the discrimination of L-Phe in the urine biological fluids was investigated. In the biological fluids, two samples from fresh human urine were collected. As shown in Table 3 , the quantitative spike-recoveries for detecting L-Phe in biological fluids ranged from 80.29% to 123.79%. Furthermore, we conducted the recovery test by spiking a series of different known concentration of L-Phe to ensure the sample test accuracy. The results have been listed in Table 3, the average recoveries of L-Phe in urine-1 sample, urine-2 sample are found to be in the ranges of 120.77–123.79%, 80.29–80.42%. Therefore, the above experiment results reveal the accuracy of the CDs@Eu(D-Cam) samples for selective discrimination of L-Phe in biological fluids [85].

In summary, we have successfully synthesized a novel ratiometric fluorescence hybrid probe CDs@Eu(D-cam) [D-cam: (1R, 3S)-(+)-Camphoric acid]. The experiment results revealed that the photo-luminescent chiral Carbon-dot@Eu(D-Cam) nano-composites could be utilized as a sensitive and selective self-calibrating probe for L-Phe. In comparison with the previous traditional L-Phe detection platforms based on a single emission mode, this detection process is based on the different responses of two emission peaks. This present approach avoids the complex modification/immobilization of fluorescent probe, and could directly detect l-Phenylalanine within a simple process. Further the current detection method can be successfully developed into the real sample discrimination of l-phenylalanine in urine. We believe that our proposed method will provide useful information for its further application in sensing other bioactive ionic species in biological and environmental sciences.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by the Foundation of Development Program of Future Expert in Tianjin Normal University (WLQR201801), the Open Project of Key Lab Adv Energy Mat Chem (Nankai Univ), 111 project (B12015), Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (Grant no. 18JCYBJC89700), Young Scientist Fund (Grant no. 21301128) and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5074).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Developing a unique metal-organic-framework-{[Cd(abtz)2(NCS)]·(ClO4)}n(abtz=1-(4-aminobenzyl)-1,2,4-triazole)as fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of ascorbic acid in biological liquid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Guo, Q. Jin, Y.Y. Wang, B. Ding, Y. Li, J.Z. Huo, X.J. Zhao， Developing a unique metal-organic-framework-{[Cd(abtz)2(NCS)]·(ClO4)}n(abtz=1-(4-aminobenzyl)-1,2,4-triazole)as fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of ascorbic acid in biological liquid, Sensors and Actuators B 234 (2016) 184-191.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-organic frameworks as platforms for functional materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Cui, B. Li, H. J. He, W. Zhou, B.L. Chen, G.D. Qian, Metal-Organic Frameworks as Platforms for Functional Materials, Acc. Chem. Res. 49 (2016) 483-493.
L.E. Kreno, K. Leong, O.K. Farha, M. Allendorf, R.P. Van Duyne, J.T. Hupp, Metal-Organic Framework Materials as Chemical, Sensors. Chem. Rev. 112 (2012) 1105-1125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-organic frameworks with functional pores for recognition of small, molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.L. Chen, S.C. Xiang, G.D. Qian, Metal-Organic Frameworks with Functional Pores for Recognition of Small, Molecules, Acc. Chem. Res. 43 (2010) 1115-1124.
J. Rocha, L.D. Carlos, F.A. Almeida Paz, D. Ananias, Luminescent Multifunctional Lanthanides-Based Metal-Organic Frameworks, Chem. Soc. Rev. 40 (2011) 926-940.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-organic frameworks: functional luminescent and photonic materials for sensing applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient fluorescence sensing of phosphate by dual-emissive lanthanide MOFs
